channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran War Has Drained U.S. Supplies of Critical, Costly Weapons
item.description: The Pentagon’s rush to rearm its Mideast forces makes it less ready to confront potential adversaries like Russia and China, administration and congressional officials say.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt and Jonathan Swan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: The remains of a university building in Tehran. Two independent groups say the U.S. expense of the war in Iran so far is between $28 billion and $35 billion.

item.title: How the Iran War Is Morphing Into a Volatile Standoff in the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The conflict has morphed into a volatile standoff in the Strait of Hormuz, as the economic costs mount and President Trump faces a political backlash at home.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural in Tehran illustrates Iran’s grip on the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: War and Sanctions Accelerate China’s Currency Push
item.description: China’s bid to build a renminbi-based financial system beyond the U.S. dollar’s reach is gaining traction as a way to sidestep sanctions.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Krolik
item.media:credit: Lincoln Agnew

item.title: Trump’s Dreams for a Battleship Led to His Navy Secretary’s Ouster
item.description: The Navy secretary, John Phelan, was supposed to deliver the first of the president’s ships by 2028. The timeline was nearly impossible.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe and Helene Cooper
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: John Phelan, the Navy secretary, in December. President Trump’s frustration over Mr. Phelan’s management of his prized battleship program grew over the past two weeks.

item.title: Trump Reposts Anti-Immigrant Tirade Calling China and India ‘Hellhole’ Places
item.description: The president touched off a furor with his post sharing a podcast episode in which the host referred to China and India as “hellhole” places.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump posted the transcript on his Truth Social account on Wednesday night.

item.title: Pentagon Fires Stars and Stripes Newspaper’s Ombudsman
item.description: The newspaper’s ombudsman, Jacqueline Smith, said she had been given no reason for her dismissal.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Valerie Plesch for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Pentagon has been taking steps to increase its editorial control of Stars and Stripes, which has covered the military continuously since World War II.

item.title: Soldier Used Classified Information to Bet on Maduro’s Ouster, U.S. Says
item.description: Federal prosecutors say that Sgt. Gannon Ken Van Dyke, who was involved in the operation to oust Nicolás Maduro from power in Venezuela, used the information to place bets on a prediction market.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser and Jonah E. Bromwich
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Nicolás Maduro, former President of Venezuela, being escorted off a helicopter to be taken to the federal courthouse in Manhattan in January.

item.title: Concern Grows Over Republican Congressman’s Mysterious Absence
item.description: A spokesman for Representative Thomas Kean Jr. said an unspecified “personal medical issue” has led the New Jersey Republican to miss House votes.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Bill Clark/CQ Roll Call, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Democrats in New Jersey were aggressively targeting Thomas Kean Jr.’s seat well before he began missing votes.

item.title: Dan Goldman Will Personally Match Each Donation as He Fights to Keep House Seat
item.description: Daniel Goldman is pledging at least $1 million of his own money, and possibly millions more, in hopes of fighting off a primary challenge from Brad Lander in New York City.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Fandos
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Daniel Goldman, who represents parts of Manhattan and Brooklyn, is one of the richest members of Congress.

item.title: DeepSeek’s Sequel Set to Extend China’s Reach in Open-Source A.I.
item.description: Chinese companies have embraced making their most advanced artificial intelligence models available to all.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Chinese start-up DeepSeek shook the industry in January 2025 with its claim that it had created an advanced A.I. system for a fraction of what its American rivals spent.

item.title: Meta to Lay Off 10 Percent of Work Force in A.I. Push
item.description: The layoffs affect about 8,000 employees, with Meta also planning to close 6,000 open roles, as the company focuses on artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac and Eli Tan
item.media:credit: Noah Berger/Associated Press
item.media:description: Meta’s headquarters in Menlo Park, Calif.

item.title: Elon Musk and Sam Altman’s Epic Fight Heads to Court
item.description: A jury trial that is set to start on Monday could shift the course of the A.I. race, with Mr. Musk seeking billions of dollars in damages from Mr. Altman’s OpenAI.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mr. Musk in February with Shivon Zilis, the mother of four of his children and a former OpenAI board member. She is expected to testify at the trial.

item.title: Intel’s Revenues Soar, Aided by A.I. Boom
item.description: The chip maker reported a 7 percent rise to $13.6 billion in its latest quarter, more than $1 billion more than Wall Street expected.
item.dc:creator: Don Clark
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Intel’s manufacturing campus in Ocotillo, Ariz., where a federally subsidized expansion is underway.

item.title: Europe Mulls What Mutual Defense Looks Like Outside NATO
item.description: European Union nations have a little-known obligation to protect one another. Experts caution it is no replacement for NATO.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger and Jeanna Smialek
item.media:credit: John Thys/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A meeting of NATO defense ministers at the group’s headquarters in Brussels in February. Europe is reconsidering its options for collective defense, as President Trump continues to threaten to leave the NATO alliance.

item.title: Saudis Withdraw Offer of Millions to Metropolitan Opera
item.description: The arrangement would have brought up to $200 million to the Metropolitan Opera, which has suffered a series of financial setbacks.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Opening night at the Metropolitan Opera in September.

item.title: A Leaner Saudi Arabia Turns From Grandiose Plans to Pragmatism
item.description: A decade after Mohammed bin Salman unveiled his “Vision 2030” program to transform the country’s economy, the kingdom is facing financial strains and reassessing its trajectory.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Saudi crown prince, Mohammed bin Salman, in Washington last fall. The prince’s plans and ambitions have grown more rapidly than the state’s financial capacity.

item.title: Touring Africa, Pope Leo Raised His Voice, but Didn’t Like the Echo
item.description: On his recent trip abroad, Leo XIV made some of his most forthright comments since becoming pope last year, but grew uncomfortable at how that criticism was interpreted.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Alberto Pizzoli/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV in Bata, Equatorial Guinea, on Wednesday.

item.title: Communication Failures Preceded Deadly Crash at LaGuardia, N.T.S.B. Says
item.description: The report focuses on the lack of transponders in the fire trucks, which investigators suggested could have allowed an automatic warning system to alert the controller of an imminent collision.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian and Patrick McGeehan
item.category: Port Authority of New York and New Jersey
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Air Canada Express jet and a fire truck collided on the runway at LaGuardia Airport last month.

item.title: Two Litigation Partners Depart Paul Weiss
item.description: Kannon Shanmugam and Masha Hansford are the latest in a string of litigators who have left the influential New York law firm.
item.dc:creator: Jessica Silver-Greenberg and Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:credit: Andrew Kelly/Reuters
item.media:description: The Paul Weiss partners Kannon Shanmugam and Masha Hansford are departing to start a practice focusing on Supreme Court and other appellate litigation at the rival firm Davis Polk.

item.title: Doug Allan, Polar Cameraman for David Attenborough’s Films, Dies at 74
item.description: He was renowned for his skill at capturing candid scenes of penguins, polar bears and other cold-weather creatures — and for his ability to tolerate extreme discomfort.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: New Gene Therapy Enables Children With a Rare Form of Deafness to Hear
item.description: The treatment, the first of its kind, was approved by the Food and Drug Administration on Thursday. “Our baby was born deaf, and now he can hear,” said one parent.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: Miles was born with otoferlin deafness, a rare form of deafness caused by a mutation in a single gene. But with a new gene therapy, he can hear.

item.title: Anne Hathaway, Queen of Effort, Is Finally Ready to Vibe
item.description: Known best for tightly wound characters in generational hit films, the actress is a tortured pop star in “Mother Mary” and returns to playful form in “The Devil Wears Prada 2.”
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli, Jon Caramanica and Thea Traff
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times

item.title: Tim Cook Did Wonders for Apple. He Also Did a Lot for Xi Jinping.
item.description: He made his company a juggernaut, but he did so by risking America’s economic and political security.
item.dc:creator: Patrick McGee
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo/Reuters

item.title: I’m One of Cuba’s Political Prisoners. When Will I Go Free?
item.description: Nearly five years in Cuban prison have taught me that the government won’t loosen its hold on power.
item.dc:creator: Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Roasts Trump at a Mock White House Correspondents’ Dinner
item.description: The late night host took “a page from the Kid Rock alternative halftime show,” which aired during the Super Bowl, in offering his own “all-American” version of the Washington tradition.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Tornado Hits Enid, Oklahoma, as Severe Weather Threatens Central U.S.
item.description: The tornado damaged homes and struck an Air Force base in Enid, Okla., the local sheriff said. Some people were injured.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado

item.title: In Britain, 7 Unelected Lords Are Helping to Block an Assisted Dying Bill
item.description: A small group in the House of Lords has proposed hundreds of amendments that are helping stymie a bill that was meant to legalize medically assisted death for the terminally ill.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Aaron Chown
item.media:description: Members of the House of Lords and House of Commons during the State Opening of Parliament, in the House of Lords, in London, in 2023.

item.title: ‘Didi vs. Modi’: A Test for the Hindu Right in India’s Bengali Heartland
item.description: The party of Prime Minister Narendra Modi has been making gains in a state once beyond its grasp, but many decry an audit that removed nine million voters, many of them Muslim.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das, Hari Kumar and Atul Loke
item.media:description: Campaign posters featuring Prime Minister Narendra Modi of India, and Mamata Banerjee, the chief minister of West Bengal, who is also known by her moniker “Didi,” in Kolkata this month.
